scenario_title: Add RR at a CNAME owner after passing prerequisites
test_rationale: This test first establishes that alias1.campus.edu. exists, then performs a single dynamic UPDATE that adds an A RR to that same owner even though the initial zone already contains a CNAME there. The prerequisites are intentionally satisfied so the only question is how the UPDATE engine applies the CNAME exclusivity rule. Different implementations may either reject the change with a YXRRSET-like error or accept it and alter the resulting rrset composition, making the difference visible in both the reply and follow-up queries.

group_1.behavior: Reject the UPDATE because the owner already has a CNAME, preserving the original alias-only state.
group_1.post_state: alias1.campus.edu. retains CNAME target.campus.edu.; no A RR appears.

group_2.behavior: Accept the UPDATE and materialize the A RR at the owner, resulting in a different post-update state than a strict CNAME-only interpretation.
group_2.post_state: alias1.campus.edu. has an A RR present; the CNAME may be removed or may coexist depending on canonicalization behavior.

expected_divergence.why_divergence: RFC 2181 §10.1 makes CNAME exclusivity a data-model rule, but RFC 2136 §3.4.2 does not prescribe a mandatory UPDATE RCODE or transaction policy for adding a non-CNAME RR to an owner that already has a CNAME. That leaves room for different servers to reject, overwrite, or transiently coexist, producing divergence in both the UPDATE reply and the observed post-state.
